subject: Chemistry
title: Why the Sky Is Blue
description: Learn Why the Sky Is Blue in one minute.

hook.text: The sky is blue because air molecules scatter short-wavelength blue light more than long-wavelength red light.

buildup.text: Lord Rayleigh proved in 1871 that scattering intensity depends on wavelength. Shorter waves scatter much more.

discovery.text: Blue light scatters 5.5 times more than red. As sunlight passes through air, blue bounces in every direction.

twist.text: At sunset, light travels through more atmosphere. Blue scatters away completely, leaving red and orange behind.

climax.text: On Mars, the sky is butterscotch and sunsets are blue—iron dust scatters red light, reversing Earth's pattern.

punchline.text: Short wavelengths scatter more. Blue fills the sky; red paints the sunset.

quiz.question: Why are sunsets red and orange?
options: Blue light scatters away during the longer path through atmosphere, leaving red
The sun changes color as it cools in the evening
Dust in the lower atmosphere emits red light
